description: A 'kirato' is a mechanism that holds a door, lid, gate, or other closure until its matching release is used. A key, latch, code, or another design may release it. 'tapu' (close) is the act of shutting the opening; the lock keeps that condition in place.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-front 'i'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip before stressed central 'a'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for unvoiced dental 't', release it, and finish on true-mid rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The whole of 'kira' (key) is audible before final 'to' brings the tongue to the teeth and rounds the word shut. That last compact contact can make the lock and its matching release easier to remember together.
usage_notes: Locks are countable. The secured thing and the person controlling release belong in the surrounding account. When the mechanism itself matters, its exact design or source term stays available beside the Phi noun.

examples.phi: mia roe kira ha kirato phae.
examples.translation: I open this lock with a key.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A lock can protect a chosen boundary or deny someone entry. Naming the mechanism leaves room to ask who controls it and whose movement it changes.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Wooden latches and fitted metal locks made household privacy a matter of visible craft long before standard hardware.

semantic_domains.creation: a fitted mechanism made to hold a closure
semantic_domains.physical: a device released by a matching key or method
semantic_domains.spatial: a mechanism controlling passage through an opening
